preamble: The debuccalization of the voiceless uvular stop /q/ to the glottal fricative /h/ represents a significant weakening of the oral constriction. The closure at the back of the mouth is lost, leaving only the friction generated at the glottis. This shift is found in specific dialects of Arabic and other languages with uvular consonants.

examples.note: In some Sa'idi dialects, the uvular stop weakens to a glottal fricative in specific contexts.
Regional variations show /q/ debuccalizing to [h], alongside other outcomes like [ɡ] or [ʔ].

sources: Watson, J. C. (2002). The Phonology and Morphology of Arabic.
Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
